sentences.text: Well, of course we had no idea what comes.
We thought maybe they would let us go back to the barracks, and maybe we can lie down.
But of course, that didn't happen.
We were dispersed.
First of all, after each counting, we were fed.
We were counted twice a day.
We lined up in the morning, and then lined up around 3 o'clock in the afternoon.
And if they found everybody, if everybody was accounted for, then we were fed.
But the first time we lined up, I remember they brought out three blankets, and laid it out in front of our lineup.
And I was looking at those blankets, I didn't know what they meant.
And I could see the blankets moving.
So after we were dispersed, after we were permitted to move around, I asked, what were those blankets they brought out, and then they took them again away?
So they told me that there were some people who came in with diabetes, and they took their insulin away from them and they were dying right in front of us.
But they had to be accounted for, so they dragged them out in the blankets and put them down in the lineup.
And then in the morning, they brought these two big barrels of so-called food.
They called it ersatz.
It was some kind of a liquid that looked like coffee or tea.
Tasted more like dishwater, but it was hot.
And we stood out there freezing half of the night.
And then I realized that not everybody's going to get some of that.
That the first prisoner was handed a bowl, and she went to the barrel, and she got some of this liquid.
Then she came back, and was going to share it with the five of us.
In the beginning, we shared.
But later on, as time went on and we were starving, whoever was up front had some of the liquid.
By the time the bowl reached the fourth and the fifth prisoner, there was nothing.
And so from then on, it became a fight every time we did the lineup.
Who's going to stand up front?
And who's going to be in the back ?
And then later we found out-- without any warning-- Dr.
Mengele arrived after lineup and was selecting for the gas chambers.
So every morning and every afternoon, you had to figure out where you want to stand.
You want to fight your way to the front of lineup, because you're so hungry that you may not make it to the afternoon lineup.
Or you will manage to survive until the afternoon, then you really want to be in the back and hide from Mengele.
In the afternoon.
In the afternoon lineup, they brought back these barrels with a similar liquid.
They called it soup.
And we received a slice of bread.
And I remember on Sundays, they gave us either a slice of horse meat salami, or they gave us a little bit of margarine.
You had to put your hands out like this.
They slapped the margarine there.
Or, occasionally, they gave us a spoonful of a marmalade, which later we found out was made of sugar beets.
Something black, like molasses.
And I will never forget the first day, when they gave us this molasses, and they slapped it into our hand, my little sister Clara went like this.
Threw it away, then she wiped her hand and shivered.
She never touched it, never touched her soup.
She couldn't drink out of that bowl that four people already handled.
And so she was starving.
The only thing she ate was the slice of bread that we received.
Nothing.
Probably, that was the worst.
And, of course, later on-- because of that-- we were filled with lice.
But water was-- we had no access to water.
Only when we went to a barrack called the latrine barrack, where we could not go individually.
We were taken collectively.
Usually the kapo and some Nazi soldiers-- again with the dogs-- taking us into their latrine barracks.
Where we had to choose whether we want to fight our way to the water-- there was water spouts in that place-- or you have to fight your way to the bathroom.
And the reason was that this wasn't happening every day.
Sometimes two, three days you didn't go to the bathroom.
So when you got into this latrine barrack, you had to decide what you want.
You want to rinse your dress out, or you want to wash your face, or you want to have a drink of water, or you want to go to the bathroom.
And the Nazi soldiers, standing around laughing at us.
Making fun of the women sitting down on the bathrooms, or standing naked, washing themselves.
I only known that this humiliation remained with me forever.
I am embarrassed when I see somebody being embarrassed.
I feel for them.
This is the kind of humiliation that destroys your humanness.
You just feel totally naked, totally vulnerable.
You knew.
Every time you went in there you knew that have the power to do anything they want to do with you.
Well, you know--
If they find you.
Not inside the barrack, but maybe somebody did it behind the barrack or something.
We were outside all day.
We were outside all day.
We were permitted to go to the barrack after the afternoon appell, to be settling down for the night.
But we were in the dust, in to dirt, in the snow, in the rain.
We were, all the time, outdoors.
No.
Auschwitz-Birkenau really was not a labor camp or work camp.
It was strictly an extermination camp, and we knew that that's we are there for, and we accepted it.
In between.
I went through once, delousing.
And that was because I was selected out by Mengele to be taken to another camp next to the C-lager where I was.
The B-lager was emptied the week before.
I'd like to tell you about that, because then B-lager-- at that time-- was occupied by midgets, non-Jewish midgets.
Where they brought in the whole families, parents and children, the grandparents.
And we were so jealous that they were permitted to stay with their parents and we were separated, not knowing where our families were.
And one night, as we were walking into our barrack to settle down, we were told that no matter what we hear, we may not come out or look out.
And that night, in the middle of the night, we could hear screaming and shooting.
And we could understand that there is an action going on in the camp next to us.
And next morning, when we woke up, we found all these midgets piled up like logs, in front of their barracks.
It was around September, at the height of the Final Solution, at the height of the exterminations.
And the crematorias were burning 24 hours a day.
And apparently there was not room in the crematoria for these people.
So they gassed them overnight, and then they were sitting there for about three days, until they were finally, slowly taken into the crematoria and disposed of.
And so, a week later, I was selected from my camp, going into the other camp.
It was then that I was separated from my sister, Clara.
And when Mengele pointed me to go to the other side, I tried to run back to my sister, because I knew she was weak.
She was very skinny.
I knew she will not take a chance.
So I ran back to her, and Mangele caught me, and handed me to the kapo.
And the kapo beat me brutally and pushed me back to the other side.
And we were separated.
But we arranged to meet at the wires, through the wires, every morning at a certain place.
So that we can wave to each other, and tell each other that we are still alive, and we're still OK.
And on Yom Kippur, in 1944-- We were worried before Yom Kippur that something is going to happen, because the Nazis always had something in store for us during holidays or on Shabbat.
And I came to the wires, and my sister didn't show.
And I thought maybe she was just upset because of the holiday.
And I thought maybe the following morning, and she didn't show then, either.
And when she didn't come the third day, I knew that something happened to her.
After the war, I found out that there were bit selections in the C-lager, and that she was selected out to be killed that day.
Yes, still, it was no different.
It was just getting-- getting busier, because the last groups of Hungarians were brought in at that time.
And the Greeks.
Salonika was emptied and the Salonikan Jews were brought in.
Some Italian Jews were brought in, and some Dutch Jews were brought in after us.
And those were the last transports.
And it was getting very busy in Auschwitz.
So then a few months later, we heard that they were going to evacuate.
Let me just tell you, in September, before my sister died, there were Americans flying overhead.
We saw these little silver dots glittering in the sunshine, and everybody yelled, the Americans!
The Americans!
And the sirens were going, and the Germans went into the bunkers and we stayed outdoors.
And we were hoping that they are going to just bomb the whole place to pieces, including us.
We didn't care.
But they didn't.
They flew over Auschwitz-Birkenau.
And then we heard bombing.
There was, about eight kilometers from us, a factory, IG Farben in Auschwitz.
And I presume that's what they were bombing.
It was an artificial rubber factory.
There's a docent of the Wiesenthal Center who was one of the pilots, and they were under express orders not to bomb Auschwitz.
I know I know.
And even if they didn't bomb Auschwitz, if they would've just bombed the railroad before it reached Auschwitz, thousands and thousands of Jews-- and others-- could have been saved.
Probably my biggest shock was really after the war, when I found out that so many non-Jews were also murdered in Auschwitz.
No, we didn't.
But we knew that the Americans were in the war, because of the planes.
And I don't know how those who told us that those were American planes, how they knew.
But there was an underground in Auschwitz.
And so, apparently--
I had contacts later with one Polish political prisoner, who was a doctor in our camp in Liebau.
Yes, that was just before they took us away.
Not to me.
The underground, of course.
One of the women who smuggled in the dynamite was hung with a few other people.
As a matter of fact, you know Freddy Diamond?
His brother was hung, and he watched it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- but to us, we just knew that there was an explosion, and we knew that the crematoria was bombed.
Yes.
We were hoping.
We really didn't have exact knowledge of it.
But we realized that something must be going on during the war, in the political war.
And so they told us that they are going to liquidate the camp.
And was sent on a death march.
Let me tell you.
That was the time that we were deloused again.
We were taken back into the showers.
The truth is that we did not know that there is a difference between the showers and the gas chambers.
And every time they took us in there, we didn't know what's going to happen to us.
And so, we feared that we are going to be finished.
It was pouring rain that day.
Icy water.
And so they took us to this shower, and then they held us outside, naked.
And we stood there from morning appell until about 4 o'clock in the afternoon, in that icy rain.
And that's when the second miracle happened.
The night before, I was burning up with fever.
I was-- I used to have tonsillitis very often as a child.
And that night, I became very ill.
And there was a mother and daughter by the name of Farkosh.
They were from Hungary.
I didn't know what happened to them after that.
And Mrs.
Farkosh, the mother, in the morning saw that I can't stand on my feet.
So she stole somewhere a blanket.
I don't know where and how.
And she wrapped me in this blanket, and she dragged me out for zahlappell.
Would she have not done it, I would have been finished that morning.
She dragged me out, and I went through the shower and the icy water.
And then I went on this death march, about 60 kilometers, where we were again put into cattle cars.
And then this cattle car, my prisoner friends surrounded me, and hugged me for body warmth.
And I arrived to the next camp, to Liebau, totally recovered.
The icy water brought my temperature down, and it was just a miracle.
I don't think so.
I don't think that's the way to cure sore throat.
[LAUGHS]
Liebau, I don't even know.
It wasn't very far, because Liebau was in Upper Silesia.
But I know that the journey lasted, again, three days.
I don't know what was this three day journey that they invented, that everything lasted three days.
Well, I was out of it.
I-- you know, I was sick, and I had temperature, and I really was worried that I'm not going to make it.
When we arrived, and I realized that I can walk-- I can stand up, I can walk-- they unloaded us at the railroad station, and we walked through this little town where they never saw a Jew before.
And we later were assigned to work at a Krupp factory.
In this group, there was about 200 of us.
And I-- I remember that the foreman, who took a liking to me afterwards, told me that the population in the town was talking about the Jews being brought to the factory.
And they said, oh, now we know how the Jews look.
With our shaved heads, and the rags that we were wearing.
They thought that's how Jews-- that's how the Jews are.
And then somebody asked-- he said somebody asked, and where are the horns?
So it is not a myth that that's what people thought of Jews.
There were four factories.
One was producing ammunition, one was producing boxes for the ammunition.
We did [NON-ENGLISH] snow chains for tanks.
And, I--
With Ukrainian slave laborers.
And there was, next door to us, a French political prison, male prison, and those French boys were also working in our factories.
Right.
And the French prisoners somehow knew what was going on.
I wouldn't be surprised if they had a radio or something, and listening to the BBC.
Knowing what was going on in the war.
So we had some news that the war is going bad for Hitler.
That's right.
That's right.
No, I don't remember doing anything nice for us for Christmas, but I remember in the height of the winter, they caught some Dutch girls hiding in the factory, not working.
And we had a kommander fuhrer who was, I don't know, 5'2'.
She was a very short, hideous woman who was mean.
No, a German SS.
And she tied these three women out-- outside, and she poured water on them and let them freeze outside.
So nobody sabotaged after that.
At least not visibly.
Well, in the last days before liberation, during the night, be heard bombing.
And we went outdoors, and we saw the Stalin candles.
These were flares which the Russians dropped in mid-air with a parachute.
And they were just hanging there in the air, lighting the whole area.
And we heard the bombing.
And again, we were hoping that that they will bomb our factories.
Because we did not know that that's the end, really.
We just knew that the Russians are close, and that they are bombing around us.
And this was going on for a few nights.
And then, the last night, there were very heavy bombing around us.
And next morning, we were not called for zahlappell to be counted.
And we were afraid to walk out, because we thought that maybe the Nazis are waiting for us with machine guns.
We were convinced that they will not let us go.
That in the last moment, they will just wipe us out.
And so we stayed indoors until later in the afternoon.
Somebody said, well, I'm going out.
I'm going to see what's going.
And we watched through the window as she walked into the middle of the platz-- zahlappelplatz.
And she raised both hands, and she said, we are free.
Apparently the French prisoners next door took a stove pipe and tied a white shirt on it.
And were marching around their prison camp, screaming so we can hear it, Nazi kaput.
Nazi kaput.
And so we ran outdoors, and we realized that the Nazis were gone.
They escaped during the night.
But we still didn't know what really was happening.
So we were worried that they may come back.
You know how war is, going back and forth.
Later in the afternoon, a Cossack on horseback-- a Russian officer-- rode in.
He looked at us, and yelled, [RUSSIAN] You're Jewish?
And we didn't know whether we should say yes or no.
We didn't know whether it's dangerous or not.
Then finally one girl said, [RUSSIAN].
This officer jumped off the horse, and came over and started to cry, and hugged us and kissed us.
And kept yelling, [RUSSIAN] I am also a Jew.
Yes.
And then he kept saying something-- this is what they did to my people-- and stayed with us.
In the afternoon, he left for a little while.
Brought in a horse, which the French soldiers shot in front of us, and made a goulash out of it.
They emptied the-- the-- the kitchen pantry, and got us potatoes.
And that was the first meal with meat and potatoes that we had.
And then he posted a guard in front of our camp and told us not to leave.
He said that the soldiers are looting and raping, and we should not leave the camp.
That he's going to consider this off limits, and he will be back when they passed through Liebau.
He came back a few days later, and he told us that we can now leave.
And there we were on our own, realizing that now we have to re-enter that world that didn't want us.
And there was no effort made by the Soviets to give help to some kind of--
Nothing.
Nothing.
And it was very dangerous.
You know, I went up to the commander [INAUDIBLE], to this officer later.
And asked him to give us a few bicycles, that maybe we can start out on a bicycle, at least.
We found out that we are very close to the Czechoslovakian border.
And I thought, once we are back in Czechoslovakia, I speak the language and the people-- I knew the Czech people.
And it's going to start changing for us.
So he gave us three bicycles with the bumashkas-- with the papers-- to tell us that the bicycle is ours.
And as we rode out for about six kilometers, a group of Russian soldiers stopped us.
And I, very cockily, took out the bumashka and showed them.
And they smiled at us, and they said, [RUSSIAN].
OK.
And took the bicycles.
And we were on our foot walking.
No, they didn't.
Apparently they must have-- I mean, the way we looked, still with the shaved heads, in this rag.
I don't think we were too desirable.
Absolutely.
Later on, it was very dangerous for a young woman to be alone.
Or even a few women walking anyplace, or being with Russians-- around Russians.
And again, I was very lucky, because I had some very close calls.
Yes.
We arrived-- we crossed the border, and we arrived to a small town.
I don't even remember the name of the town.
And I walked into a restaurant.
And the restaurateur looked at us, and asked me in Czech where-- where do we come from?
